metadata.summary: Turn web apps into installable PWAs with offline capability via Workbox, Web App Manifest, and user-driven prompts. Never silent skipWaiting.

content.title: PWA Development
content.summary: Methodology for turning a web app into an installable Progressive Web App with offline capability via Workbox service workers, a compliant Web App Manifest, and a user-driven install/update prompt. Core rule: default to registerType: 'prompt' — never silent skipWaiting() for app shells with in-flight state; ship a working offline.html from day one.

why.p: PWAs combine the reach of the web with near-native install and offline UX. Service-worker bugs are sticky — clients can be locked on a broken SW for weeks — so the update handshake must be explicit and tested. Workbox's per-resource caching strategies (NetworkFirst for APIs, CacheFirst for images) prevent stale data from killing freshness.

list.item: Adding installability and offline support to an existing SPA
Mobile-first apps where skipping the App Store gate is valuable
Content sites needing fast repeat-visit loads via precaching
Apps needing push notifications without a native rewrite
Internal tools that must feel native on field devices and survive flaky networks
Background-heavy apps needing native features (Bluetooth LE, geofencing, CallKit)
iOS-first products where push and install UX still lag (iOS Web Push only since 16.4)
Static marketing sites with low return rate — SW cost outweighs benefit
Apps requiring exact filesystem access, USB/HID, or low-level audio — use Capacitor/Tauri
Single-session tools (one-time forms) where caching adds risk without payoff

rule.statement: Manifest must include name, short_name, start_url, display: standalone, and icon entries for 192px and 512px with a maskable variant for Android adaptive icons.
rule.rationale: Missing any of these fields fails the installability check. The 512px maskable icon is required for the Android adaptive icon system; without it, install is blocked on some devices.

rule.statement: Use NetworkFirst for API responses and HTML navigation; CacheFirst for images and fonts; StaleWhileRevalidate for JS/CSS static assets.
rule.rationale: CacheFirst on APIs returns stale data indefinitely. NetworkFirst with a networkTimeoutSeconds fallback gives fresh data when online and cached data when offline.

example.title: WRONG - CacheFirst on API returns stale data indefinitely
example.code: // WRONG - CacheFirst on API returns stale data indefinitely registerRoute(({ url }) => url.pathname.startsWith('/api/'), new CacheFirst());

example.title: CORRECT - NetworkFirst with 10s timeout for API

rule.statement: Register the SW from the same origin as the app; handle updatefound events to notify users of new versions rather than silently calling skipWaiting.
rule.rationale: Silent skipWaiting terminates in-flight requests and can corrupt client state mid-session. An explicit user-driven reload preserves UX.

example.title: Service worker registration with update detection

rule.statement: Pre-cache and serve /offline.html from the SW install event; register it as the fallback for all failed navigations.
rule.rationale: Without an offline fallback, failed navigations show a browser error page — the service worker makes the experience worse than having no SW at all.

rule.statement: Defer the install prompt (preventDefault on beforeinstallprompt) and show it only on a deliberate user action, not immediately on page load.
rule.rationale: Showing install prompts immediately has very low acceptance rates and annoys users who are still evaluating the app. Capture the event, show a banner after meaningful engagement.

example.title: useInstallPrompt React hook

rule.statement: Set registerType: 'prompt' in vite-plugin-pwa to prevent automatic skipWaiting; always include a navigateFallback pointing to /offline.html.
rule.rationale: registerType: 'autoUpdate' calls skipWaiting silently, potentially breaking in-flight state. 'prompt' gives the app control over when the update activates.

rule.statement: Test explicitly on Safari iOS: Background Sync and Push Notifications have limited support; storage is evicted after approximately 7 days of inactivity.
rule.rationale: Agents typically test only on Chromium and ship features that silently fail on iOS (the majority of mobile web traffic for many products).

antipattern.description: LLMs default to CacheFirst everywhere; that breaks freshness for APIs and HTML.
antipattern.reason: Review per-route strategy. CacheFirst is only appropriate for long-lived assets like images and fonts.

antipattern.description: Agents forget the offline fallback registration in the SW install event, leaving navigations dead offline.
antipattern.reason: Always register /offline.html in the SW and use it as the navigateFallback for all navigations.

antipattern.description: Service-worker scope errors: registering at /app/sw.js but expecting root scope /.
antipattern.reason: SW scope is limited to the directory of the SW file; /app/sw.js cannot cache / paths. Place SW at root.

antipattern.description: iOS gotcha: agents test on Chromium and miss Safari quirks (no Background Sync, limited Push, storage caps).
antipattern.reason: Test explicitly on Safari iOS with DevTools to verify functionality. Do not assume iOS behavior matches Chrome.

antipattern.description: Push: agents generate VAPID keys but skip the subscription persistence layer.
antipattern.reason: Require an explicit DB schema diff for storing push subscriptions. Push endpoints must be persisted and renewed.

section.name: Agentic-Workflow
section.p: A two-pass agent works well: first an audit pass runs Lighthouse + a manifest/SW checklist and emits a gap list; second pass implements the missing pieces (manifest, Workbox config, install prompt hook, update banner). Treat the service worker as a deploy artifact — version it explicitly and gate releases on a smoke test that loads, mutates cache, then forces an update.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — design → impl → verify with Lighthouse gate
A custom pwa-auditor (haiku) — runs Lighthouse + manifest validation and produces gap list
A custom sw-update-flow (sonnet) — designs the skipWaiting/clients.claim handshake and the user-facing reload UX

section.name: CLI-Tools
section.p: lighthouse — PWA audit, perf, a11y. Install: npm i -g lighthouse. Docs: https://github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse
pwa-asset-generator — Generate icons + splash screens from one source. Install: npm i -g pwa-asset-generator
workbox-cli — Generate/inject SW manifest. Install: npm i -g workbox-cli. Docs: https://developer.chrome.com/docs/workbox/modules/workbox-cli
vite-plugin-pwa / @angular/pwa / next-pwa (Serwist) — Framework-side SW + manifest plumbing. Docs: https://vite-pwa-org.netlify.app · https://serwist.pages.dev
web-push — Send VAPID push from CLI/Node. Install: npm i web-push
pwabuilder CLI — Build native wrappers (TWA/MSIX) from PWA. https://www.pwabuilder.com
bubblewrap — Generate Trusted Web Activity Android APKs. Install: npm i -g @bubblewrap/cli
unlighthouse — Crawl-wide Lighthouse runs. Install: npm i -g @unlighthouse/cli

section.name: Services-and-Apps
section.p: Firebase Cloud Messaging (SaaS) — Push backend; REST API for sending. Agent-friendly.
OneSignal (SaaS) — Higher-level push + segmentation, REST API. Agent-friendly.
web-push (Node) + own VAPID (OSS) — Self-host push; agents store keys in secret manager. Agent-friendly.
Workbox (OSS) — First-class SW abstractions; declarative config. Agent-friendly.
Serwist (OSS) — Workbox successor for Next.js; TS-first. Agent-friendly.
PWABuilder (SaaS) — Generates store packages from a PWA URL. Agent-friendly.
Cloudflare Workers + Cache API (SaaS) — Edge-side complement to client SW; not a replacement. Partial agent support.
Sentry / LogRocket (SaaS) — Capture SW errors and offline UX regressions. Agent-friendly.

list.item: Default to registerType: 'prompt' and explicit user-driven reload — never silent skipWaiting() for app shells with in-flight state
Ship a working offline.html from day one; without it, the SW makes failures worse
Cache APIs with NetworkFirst + a short networkTimeoutSeconds — CacheFirst on data is a footgun
Version your SW filename or use cacheName suffixes; old caches must be cleaned in activate
Test with Chrome DevTools "Offline" and "Slow 3G" toggles in CI via lighthouse-ci, not just locally
Keep manifest icons at all required sizes (192, 512) plus a maskable variant for Android adaptive icons
For installability: serve over HTTPS, register SW from same origin, manifest must include start_url, display, name, icons[192], icons[512]
LLMs default to CacheFirst everywhere; that breaks freshness for APIs and HTML — review per-route strategy
Agents forget the offline fallback registration in the SW install event, leaving navigations dead offline
Service-worker scope errors: registering at /app/sw.js but expecting root scope / — fail at runtime, not build
iOS gotcha: agents test on Chromium and miss Safari quirks (no Background Sync, limited Push, storage caps)
Push: agents generate VAPID keys but skip the subscription persistence layer; require an explicit DB schema diff
Human-in-loop checkpoint: SW deploys are sticky — promote to prod behind a kill switch and a back-out SW that unregisters cleanly
Agents forget that DevTools "Update on reload" hides real-user upgrade flow bugs; require a manual real-device pass

template.purpose: Complete Web App Manifest with required fields and icon sizes
Minimal Vite PWA plugin config with NetworkFirst API caching
